query: effect of McRoberts maneuver on obturator nerve

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: BLT/LAS Procedure - Pelvic Dysfunctions -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The proper sacral motion requires the use of various ligaments such as the sacroiliac ligament, which connects the sacrum to the ilium and limits excessive anterior and inferior sacral motion. The sacrotuberous ligament comes together with the posterior sacroiliac ligament to function in resisting the nutation of the sacrum during normal gait. [2] The greater and lesser sciatic foramen house the sciatic nerve, superior and inferior gluteal nerve, pudendal nerve, posterior femoral cutaneous nerve, nerve to quadratus femoris, and nerve to internal obturator muscle as well as several major vessels such as the superior and inferior gluteal artery and vein along with the internal pudendal artery and vein. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: BLT/LAS Procedure - Pelvic Dysfunctions -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Another study looking at the effects of OMT on hospital length-of-stay and incidence of postoperative ileus in general surgical patients showed that OMT decreased time to flatus and decreased length-of-stay. [8] The anatomy that is targeted with OMT in the pelvis can be thought of in terms of autonomics, biomechanics, and circulation. Autonomic tone can be modulated through manipulation of the sacrum. The pelvic splanchnic nerves arise from S2-S4 nerve roots and carry afferent parasympathetic nerve fibers to the distal end of the transverse colon, descending colon, sigmoid colon, rectum, cervix, and upper vagina. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Articulatory Procedure - Sacral Dysfunctions -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Osteopathic manipulative treatment is a useful tool in correcting somatic dysfunctions to optimize the biomechanics of the sacrum as well as improve the autonomic outflow from the sacral nerves. OMT is a safe, effective, and efficient treatment option for patients with sacral dysfunctions. [9] Specifically, sacral rocking is a gentle articulatory technique that accentuates the sacrum’s normal physiologic respiratory motion; it can be useful in correcting any respiratory restrictions of the sacrum. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Peripheral Nerve Blocks -- Technique or Treatment -- Obturator nerve block
retrieved_docs.content: The patient is placed in the supine position with the leg externally rotated. The femoral vein is identified using an ultrasound probe placed in the inguinal crease. The probe is then moved medially to visualize the pectineus and adductor longus muscles. The needle is inserted in-plane or out of plane and is directed to the fascial plane between the adductor brevis and magnus, and 5 mL to 10 mL of local anesthetic is injected. Before injection, however, aspiration should be performed to ensure no blood return is noted.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Obturator Nerve -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Just as the obturator nerve can become compressed by a hernia, the obturator nerve can become compressed by fascia or other tissue in the pelvis. Specifically, the nerve can become entrapped at the exit of the obturator canal or more distally by fascia. Entrapment of the obturator nerve causes exercise-induced medial thigh pain, typically in athletes. Hip abduction and extension aggravate the pain, whereas resisted adduction does not elicit pain. Conservative treatment has not been the target of much research, but stretching and strengthening exercises, cognitive behavioral education, neural gliding, and manual therapy may have utility in easing symptoms. Neurolysis or neurectomy may merit consideration if symptoms are refractory to treatment. [12] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: McRoberts Maneuver -- Technique or Treatment -- The McRoberts Maneuver
retrieved_docs.content: The maneuver is usually attempted for approximately 30 seconds. If delivery has not occurred during this time with gentle traction on the fetal head, the team should move on to other maneuvers (eg, suprapubic pressure, delivery of the posterior arm). [1] Prolonged or sustained use of the position should be avoided due to the potential risk of maternal lower extremity neuropathy from femoral nerve compression. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Ligaments -- Clinical Significance -- Pudendal Nerve Entrapment
retrieved_docs.content: The pudendal vessels and nerves pass behind the sacrospinous ligament. The pudendal nerve may get trapped between the sacrotuberous and sacrospinous ligaments. [27] This may lead to perineal pain, genital numbness, and incontinence (both urinary and fecal). The main symptom of pudendal nerve entrapment is severe pain while sitting. [28] Because of this, pudendal nerve entrapment is called bicyclist syndrome. If needed, special imaging studies, such as an MRI of the pudendal nerves, may be ordered. Temporary relief may be obtained via an ultrasound-guided pudendal nerve block. In those without response following this intervention, surgery may help relieve symptoms by decompression. A randomized control trial demonstrated that surgical decompression is efficacious and safe in patients with pudendal nerve entrapment unresponsive to conservative measures with analgesia and nerve blocks. [29] The trial found that among 16 patients, 71% had experienced improved symptoms following surgical decompression, which was associated with no surgical complications, and the improvement in symptoms persisted at 4-year follow-up in three-quarters of patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Articulatory Procedure - Sacral Dysfunctions -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: The physician should discuss the procedure with the patient and explain all risks and benefits. The patient must understand precisely what the clinician will be doing and gives consent before proceeding. Additionally, the physician should ensure that the patient is comfortable lying prone. Before performing OMT, the physician should palpate the motion of the sacrum to compare the motion after completion of the treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Muscle Energy and Counterstrain Procedure - Psoas Muscle Procedures -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The psoas major and minor muscles receive innervation of the L1-3 branches of the lumbar plexus, while the iliacus receives innervation from the femoral nerve. [7] The psoas and the iliacus muscle fibers pass deep to the inguinal ligament and merge to insert on the less trochanter of the femur. The iliopsoas is primarily responsible for hip flexion but also aids in external rotation of the leg and may also act as a stabilizer of the spine during hip flexion. A study done by Hu et al. found that the contralateral psoas muscle was activated with hip flexion and is likely involved in spine stabilization. [8] OMT is used to treat iliopsoas somatic dysfunctions to decrease pain, improve range of motion, and restore neuromusculoskeletal function.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteopathic Manipulative Treatment: Articulatory Procedure - Sacral Dysfunctions -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Nerves from the S2-S4 spinal levels leave the spinal canal via the ventral rami of the sacrum. The nerve roots form pelvic splenic nerves, which serve to innervate the lower gastrointestinal tract, urinary tract, and sexual organs. [7] Thus, somatic dysfunctions of the sacrum have the potential to impede proper innervation of these organs. Articulatory techniques (ART) make up one category of techniques that have been shown effective in treating the sacral somatic dysfunctions.  These techniques are low-velocity and low-amplitude; they include a passive, direct force from the physician through a restrictive barrier. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Bladder Cancer -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Effective elimination of the obturator reflex can be done by neuromuscular blockade during general anesthesia or using an obturator nerve block. [158] [161] Spinal anesthesia alone will not block the obturator reflex. [159] Performing an obturator nerve block when using spinal anesthesia for a TURBT will reduce the incidence of obturator reflex reactions and bladder perforations. [162] [163]

retrieved_docs.title: Sacral Neuromodulation -- Technique or Treatment -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: During sacral neuromodulation lead placement, a foramen needle is angled at 60° and inserted into the S3 foramen. The desired motor response includes dorsiflexion of the great toe and contraction in the perineum and anus, known as the bellows reflex. Sensory responses may include fluttering or vibration sensations or pulling sensations in the scrotum, vagina, perineum, or inner gluteal fold. In addition, the clinician must be familiar with S2 and S4 nerve root responses, as they may not result in optimal therapy outcomes. Stimulation of the S2 root typically induces pinching of the anal sphincter, plantar flexion, and lateral foot rotation, accompanied by sensations in the leg and buttock area. In contrast, stimulation of the S4 root triggers the bellows reflex without lower extremity movement and elicits a pulling sensation around the perineum. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2679
retrieved_docs.content: The retroperitoneální prostor is a key anatomical region involved in both imaging and interventional procedures. A diagnostic ultrasound of the abdomen or retroperitoneum directly targets the retroperitoneální prostor, providing real‑time visualization of structures within this space. Conversely, the removal of a tube from the retroperitoneum is performed as an indirect procedure related to the retroperitoneální prostor, reflecting its role in therapeutic interventions that access or modify contents of this deep abdominal compartment. Together, these relationships underscore the retroperitoneální prostor’s importance as both a direct site for diagnostic imaging and an indirect site for procedural management.

Insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts—including the intraperitoneal ventricular shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) and shunts to the nasopharynx—are essential for managing ventricular enlargement or hydrocephalus. Systemic nervous system disorders are broadly related to vascular headache, illustrating how anatomical sites, disease classifications, clinical similarities, and surgical treatments are interwoven in the comprehensive understanding and care of nervous system disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative methods. It includes arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, several types of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty with or without cholangiography), exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial, laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and disc excision at a single lumbar interspace, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration combined with mediastinal approaches such as sternal split, transthoracic access, or cervical mediastinal exploration. Excision also serves as the methodological basis for other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as for percutaneous nephrolithotomy (perkutánní nefrolitotomie). In addition, Excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating its relevance to reconstructive nasal surgery. Certain extensive spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, namely laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy when more than two vertebral segments are involved in either the cervical or lumbar spine. The hierarchical structure places Clinical actions A‑G as a parent group under Excision, and Excision is also referenced in a colorectal‑cancer diagnostic protocol, highlighting its role in broader clinical pathways. Overall, Excision functions as a central concept that unites diverse operative techniques while delineating specific exclusions and connections to related procedural domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 684
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration procedures constitute a broad operative category in which direct visual or instrumental assessment of an anatomic region is performed, often together with therapeutic interventions, and are organized under the parent clinical‑action set “Clinical actions A‑G.” In the endocrine system, adrenalectomy—partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, dorsally, or via a lumbar approach, with or without biopsy—serves as an exploration procedure and may include excision of adjacent retroperitoneal tumors. Hepatobiliary exploration comprises cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and may be combined with cholangiography, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, followed by choledochotomy or choledochostomy for drainage, calculus removal, or cholecystotomy. Neurosurgical exploration includes cranial procedures such as exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) or supratentorial regions,
